coredata.dc:title: White pepper-derived ratiometric carbon dots for highly selective detection and imaging of coenzyme A
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Ruiqing
Guo, Ying
Xie, Lianwu
Shi, Shuyun
Xu, Jinju
Tong, Chaoying
Lin, Qinlu
Li, Te
coredata.dc:description: A new-style white pepper derived dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) with a quantum yield of 10.4% was designed and facile constructed with one-pot solvothermal method. The green emission (520 nm) had an efficient and special “turn-on” fluorescence sensing of coenzyme A (CoA) with the aid of Cu2+, while red emission (668 nm) barely changed and worked as reference. In the concentration range (0–150 µM), relative fluorescence intensity ratios (F520/F668) showed excellent linear correlation with concentrations of CoA, and detection limit was as low as 8.75 nm. Moreover, the strategy has been successfully applied for label-free detection of CoA in real pig liver samples with good recoveries (93.3–108.0%). Notably, the synthesized CDs had durable fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility for cellular imaging, which demonstrated wide and promising applicability for biosensing and bioimaging in the future.
coredata.dcterms:subject: White pepper
Carbon dots
Ratiometric nanosensor
Coenzyme A
Cellular imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Mechanism of off-on ratiometric CoA sensing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Foundation for Innovative Research Groups of the National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Procedures for synthesis of white pepper-derived ratiometric CDs.

UV–vis spectrum (black line) and fluorescence spectrum of CDs (red line) (A), fluorescence spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths (B). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Fluorescence spectra of CDs-Cu2+ after addition of different concentrations of CoA (A); linear relationship between F520/F668 and the concentrations of CoA (B).

Fluorescence images of HeLa cells. (A) Incubated with CDs; (B) Incubated with CDs-Cu2+. (C) Pretreated with CoA and then incubated with CDs-Cu2+. Excitation wavelength: 420 nm, collection wavelength 525–550 nm for green channel, and ≥560 nm for red channel. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: White pepper-derived ratiometric carbon dots for highly selective detection and imaging of coenzyme A

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new-style white pepper derived dual-emission carbon dots (CDs) with a quantum yield of 10.4% was designed and facile constructed with one-pot solvothermal method. The green emission (520 nm) had an efficient and special “turn-on” fluorescence sensing of coenzyme A (CoA) with the aid of Cu2+, while red emission (668 nm) barely changed and worked as reference. In the concentration range (0–150 µM), relative fluorescence intensity ratios (F520/F668) showed excellent linear correlation with concentrations of CoA, and detection limit was as low as 8.75 nm. Moreover, the strategy has been successfully applied for label-free detection of CoA in real pig liver samples with good recoveries (93.3–108.0%). Notably, the synthesized CDs had durable fluorescence, low cytotoxicity, and good biocompatibility for cellular imaging, which demonstrated wide and promising applicability for biosensing and bioimaging in the future.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence probes have attracted great attentions for their simple, rapid, sensitive, selective, and real-time chemo/biosensing and bioimaging of target ions, molecule, protein, and DNA (Guo & Zhao, 2019). To date, a variety of fluorescence probes, such as fluorescence dyes, fluorescence proteins, fluorescence inorganic or organic nanoparticles, have been successfully developed and applied (Li et al., 2019; Long, Li, Tong, Wu, & Shi, 2019; Mintz, Zhou, & Leblanc, 2019). During the fabricated fluorescence probes, carbon dots (CDs), a new type of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have been recently received extensive interests because of their easy preparation procedures, unique optical properties, high physical, chemical and photostability, low biological toxicity and excellent biocompatibility for biomedical applications (Arvand & Mirroshandel, 2019; Granados, Thangarasu, Singh, & Vázquez-Ramos, 2019). Recently, natural biomasses, such as cabbage (Alam, Park, Ghouri, Park, & Kim, 2015), date kernel (Amin, Afkhami, Hosseinzadeh, & Madrakian, 2018), rose-heart radish (Liu et al., 2017), milk (Wang & Zhou, 2014), spinach (Li et al., 2017), leek (Shi et al., 2016), garlic (Zhao et al., 2015), corn bract (Zhao et al., 2017), are extensively acknowledged to be a kind of green, economic and sustainable precursors to prepare CDs. Moreover, natural biomasses always contain a large number of nitrogenous compounds (e.g. proteins, amino acid, alkaloid), which is feasible to achieve good nitrogen (N) doping to improve the quantum yield (QY), and fluorescence properties of CDs (Hu et al., 2017). And complex components in natural biomasses also make the fabricated CDs contain different surface functional groups for special sensing properties (Amin et al., 2018; Liu et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017).

Most of the reported CDs are synthesized by hydrothermal treatment with single-emissive modulation type, which are sometimes easily disturbed by the changes of sample matrix, instrumental efficiency, and inhomogeneous distribution of fluorescence probe (Li et al., 2017; Shi et al., 2016). To overcome these shortages, CDs based ratiometric fluorescence probes, having a built-in self-rectification calibration, are therefore developed to determine the ratio of fluorescence intensities at two or more well-resolved emission bands, which could improve quantitative accuracy, and enhance imaging contrast and resolution (Xiang et al., 2018). In the past few years, a great deal of efforts have been dedicated to the fabrication of CDs based ratiometric fluorescence probes, and most of them are combining single-emissive CDs with other fluorescence materials (e.g. CDs, semiconductor quantum dots, gold/silver nanoparticles, silica nanoparticles, organic dyes) through embedding, covalent bonds formation, electrostatic interaction, or simply mixing (Liu et al., 2019; Xiang et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2017). Recently, in order to simplify preparation procedures, several researchers have developed one-pot synthesis process to prepare CDs based ratiometric fluorescence probes. For example, Song and coworkers prepared dual-emissive CDs by a one-pot hydrothermal carbonization of o-phenylenediamine and phosphoric acid for response of pH and lysine (Song et al., 2017). Wang et al. carbonized glutathione in water/formamide mixture at room temperature to prepare dual-emission CDs for the detection of Fe3+ and Zn2+ in different pH environments (Wang, Lao, Ding, Zhang, & Liu, 2019). It was noted that natural biomasses are rich of organic compounds, including non-fluorescence compounds and fluorescence dyes (e.g. chlorophyll, carotenoid, indigo), therefore, CDs using natural biomasses as the starting materials might display dual fluorescence emissions (i.e. intrinsic structure of CDs, and surface group originated from fluorescence dye) through selection of appropriate reaction solvent (Li et al., 2017; Zhao et al., 2017). To our best knowledge, reports about using natural biomasses to generate CDs based ratiometric fluorescence probes remain rare.

Coenzyme A (CoA), a kind of natural important coenzyme existence in human, animal and plant, is related to the energy provided, acyl transfer, immune activation and many biomedical reactions (Nagana Gowda, Abell, & Tian, 2019). It is commonly used in clinical practice to form “energy mixture” for the adjuvant treatment of chronic diseases such as nephrotic syndrome, uremia, chronic heparin (Shibata, Nakai, & Fukuwatari, 2012). Moreover, it was an important factor regulating sugar, fat and protein metabolism. Owing to its importance, several methods have been developed to reliably, accurately and sensitively determine CoA in biological samples, including high performance liquid chromatography, proton magnetic resonance, electrochemistry, spectrophotometry, and fluorescence (Hu et al., 2017; Shibata et al., 2012; Wu et al., 2016; Zhao, Kong, Liu, & Wang, 2018). To our best knowledge, few reports fabricated CDs for CoA detection. Zhao's group synthesized single-emissive S and N co-doped carbon dots by water chestnut and onion for the determination and imaging of CoA (Hu et al., 2017). Cui et al. extracted chlorophyll from pakchoi, which was then combined with polyoxyethylene bisamine for preparation of ratiometric CDs for imaging CoA in living cells (Cui, Liu, Ye, & Zhao, 2019). Thus, it is meaningful to establish ratiometric CDs to selectively and sensitively detect CoA.

White pepper, a commonly used seasoning and condiment rich in natural alkaloids and porphyrin derivatives (e.g. chlorophyll, Vitamin B12) (Guo, Peng, Tong, Shi, & Liu, 2018), was selected as raw material to prepare ratiometric CDs. Prepared CDs presented two strong fluorescence emissions at 520 and 668 nm under a single excitation at 420 nm. The emission around 668 nm, similar fluorescence emission with that of porphyrin derivatives, might be from the surface porphyrin groups, which was seldom affected by metal ions and amino acids. The emission at about 520 nm might originate from the intrinsic structure of CDs, which could be quenched by Cu2+ and then recovered by CoA. Thus, the obtained CDs could be used for ratiometric detection of CoA in real sample analysis. In addition, the prepared CDs are proved to have excellent cellular imaging capability.

White pepper was purchased from local supermarket (Changsha, China), which was harvested from Haikou, Hainan, China, and identified as white Piper nigrum L. by Prof. Mijun Peng, Guangdong Institute of Analysis, Guangzhou, China. Polyoxyethylenebis (amine) (H2N-PEG-H2N), NaH2PO4·H2O, Na2HPO4·12H2O, NaOH, CoSO4·7H2O, AgNO3, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, MgSO4·7H2O, CaCl2·2H2O, SrCl2·6H2O, HgCl2, FeCl3·6H2O, Mn(CH3COO)2·4H2O, Cr(CH3COO)3, LiCl, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, FeCl2·4H2O and anhydrous ethanol were supplied by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). CoA, cysteine (Cys), homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH), tyrosine (Tyr), leucine (Leu), alanine (Ala), tryptophan (Trp), threonine (Thr), phenylalanine (Phe), lysine (Lys), isoleucine (Ile), methionine (Met), serine (Ser), valine (Val), methylthiazolyldiphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT), dopamine (DA), dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) were acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents used in this study were of analytical grade without additional purification, and ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm) was used.

Tecnai G2 20S-twin transmission electron microscope (TEM, FEI, Prague, Czech) was employed to characterize the morphology of CDs. Particle size and polydispersity of microspheres were obtained in the particle size analysis (PSA) (Malvern Instruments Ltd., Malvern, UK). Functional properties on the surface were determined by PHI Quantera II X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc., Waltham, MA). The lattice of CDs was investigated by Rigaku D/max2500v/pc X-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan). IR Prestige-21 Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) (Shimadzu, Japan) was introduced to record the infrared spectra. Fluorescence properties were studied by a Perkin-Elmer LS-55 luminescence spectrometer (PerkinElmer, USA) with an excitation wavelength at 420 nm. The excitation and emission silts were set at 10 nm and 20 nm, respectively. UV measurements were carried out on a UV-2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Fluo Time 100 (PicoQuant, Germany) was used to analyze the fluorescence lifetime. Enzyme linked immunosorbent assay reader (BioTek Instrument Inc., Winooski, USA) was used in MTT assay. Cell imaging was studied using Snap cool HQ2 imaging system (Photometrics Inc., USA). The pH environments were supplied by Five Easy plus pH Meter (Mettler Toledo, China).

Ratiometric CDs were prepared by refluxing white pepper in anhydrous ethanol under nitrogen protection (Fig. 1 ). Briefly, white pepper was dried at 60 °C, crushed, and sieved (500 mesh). Afterward, powdered white pepper (2.0 g) was dispersed in anhydrous ethanol (40.0 mL) in a round-bottom flask (100 mL), and then refluxed in water bath for 24 h within nitrogen protection. Then the supernatant was cooled down to room temperature and filtered using a 0.45 μm membrane. Subsequently, H2N-PEG-NH2 (1.2 mg mL−1)/anhydrous ethanol solution (10.0 mL) was added into the filtrate, and continuously refluxed at 75 °C for 4 h. After being filtered with a 0.45 μm membrane again, the obtained filtrate was dialyzed in water (MWCO = 1000) for 48 h. Finally, resulting CDs were frozen dried and stored at 4 °C for further use.

All detections were carried out with the excitation wavelength at 420 nm, and the concentration of PBS buffer was 100 mM, with pH at 7.4. Firstly, 6.0 mg as-synthesized CDs were dispersed in 3.0 mL PBS buffer. The selective sensing of CDs to Cu2+ was investigated by adding fourteen different metal ions (30.0 μL, 0.04 mM, Cu2+, Li+, Sr2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Fe3+, Ag+, Mn2+, Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Hg2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Fe2+). The “CDs-Cu2+” probe was obtained by adding 30.0 µL Cu2+ (6.5 mM) into CDs solution (3.0 mL, 2.0 mg mL−1). After incubation at room temperature for 15 min, different concentrations of CoA were added. After incubation for 5 min at room temperature, the fluorescence spectra were measured. The selectivity property of CDs-Cu2+ to CoA was evaluated by adding different potent interferences (30.0 μL, 1.0 mΜ, Hcy, GSH, Cys, Tyr, Leu, Ala, Trp, Thr, Phe, Lys, Ile, Met, Ser, Val, DA, CoA). Taking the value of F668 as reference, F520/F668 values and CoA concentrations were collected to plot the calibration curve.

Real sample measurements were carried out in pig liver samples. In briefly, fresh pig liver samples were purchased from local market, Changsha, China. 4.0 g of each pig liver was minced into liver homogenates in 10.0 mL PBS buffer, which was then pretreated with those described in literature (Hu et al., 2017). Finally, each pig live sample solution was concentrated and dissolved in 1.0 mL of buffer solution, and then 30.0 µL was added into 3.0 mLof CDs solution (2.0 mg mL−1 CDs and 65.0 μM Cu2+). After incubation for 5 min, fluorescence spectra were recorded.

MTT assay was employed for evaluation of the cytotoxicity of CDs. Firstly, HeLa cells were seeded in 96-well plate and attached for 24 h in cell culture medium with the density of 1 × 104 cells per well. After removing cell culture medium and washing with PBS buffer three times, HeLa cells were treated with CDs with final concentrations of 0, 0.05, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4, 0.6, 0.8, and 1.0 mg mL−1 for 24 h. After that, each well was washed with 150.0 μL of PBS buffer three times and then added with freshly prepared MTT solution (150.0 µL, 0.5 mg mL−1). After being incubated for another 4 h, supernatant was removed and 150.0 µL of DMSO was added and shaken for 15 min. The optical density (OD) of each well was detected at 490 nm. Cell viability was then calculated as following: cell viability (%) = (ODtreated/ODcontrol) × 100%, where ODtreated and ODcontrol were obtained with and without treatment with CDs.

Cell imaging property of CDs was investigated as follows. HeLa cells were incubated in a 35 mm culture dish at 37 °C for 24 h. After removing culture medium and washing with PBS buffer three times, Hela cells were incubated with 1.5 mL of fresh culture medium containing CDs (1.0 mg mL−1) for another 3.0 h. The extracellular medium was removed, and cells were washed with PBS buffer. Subsequently, cells were re-incubated with 1.5 mL of Cu2+ solution (100.0 µM) for 30 min and imaged. For identification of CoA in cells, HeLa cells were firstly incubated with CoA solution (150.0 µM) for 1 h, and then washed with PBS solution three time. Then CDs-Cu2+ solution (1.0 mg mL−1 for CDs and 100.0 µM for Cu2+) was added and pretreated for 3.0 h for confocal imaging.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of CDs were measured to investigate the optical properties of CDs. Previous research found that CDs displayed similar UV adsorption bands, fluorescence emission, and detection properties with some of the starting materials (Shuanguan et al., 2016). Here, ethanol, a solvent usually used to extract multi-compounds from food/natural products (Shi et al., 2019), was selected as reaction solvent. Chemical compositions of white pepper have been analyzed by our group using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) method (Fig. S1) (Guo et al., 2018). Therefore, large number of compounds in white pepper (e.g. alkaloids, porphyrin derivatives) could be efficiently extracted and acted as carbon and nitrogen source to prepare CDs (Guo et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 2 A, CDs showed strong UV absorption peaks at about 261, 310, 343 nm and a weak one at 665 nm. Absorption peak at 261 nm could be ascribed to the π–π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains. Absorption peaks at 310 and 343 was similar to the characteristic absorptions of alkaloids (e.g. piperine) in white pepper (Guo et al., 2018), which could deduce that nitrogen was successfully doped in CDs. And absorption peak at 665 nm might be attributed to the Q-band of porphyrin derivatives. In the fluorescence measurement (Fig. 2A), CDs exhibited two emission peaks at 520 and 668 nm when the excitation wavelength was 420 nm. The fluorescence intensities at 520 and 668 nm increased with the increase of excitation wavelength from 400 to 420 nm, and then decreased when excitation wavelength increased to 440 nm (Fig. 2B). The fluorescence emission at 520 nm exhibited a red shift excitation-dependent manner during the excitation wavelengths increasing from 400 to 440 nm, while fluorescence emission at 668 nm exhibited excitation-independent behavior by changing excitation wavelength from 400 to 420 nm, and then presented a blue shift from 420 to 440 nm. Moreover, the emission peak at 668 nm was shorter than that of porphyrin derivatives (usually over 670 nm). As a result, it could be deduced porphyrin derivatives were conjugated with CDs. Therefore, the emission peaks at 520 nm and 668 nm were from the intrinsic structure of CDs and the surface porphyrin derivatives, respectively, and the different particle size and diverse emissive trap sites led to the excitation-dependent phenomenon (Hu et al., 2017).

Ethanol volume (20, 30, 40, 60, 80 and 120 mL) and refluxing time (16, 20, 24 and 28 h), two main factors for extract efficiency of white pepper (2.0 g), were investigated in synthesis procedures. It was observed that fluorescence intensities of two emission peaks slightly increased when ethanol volume improved from 20 to 40 mL, possibly as a result of the increased extraction efficiency, and then remained invariable (Fig. S2A). Both fluorescence intensities increased slightly and reached the apexes when refluxing time increased from 16 to 24 h (Fig. S2B). Finally, ethanol (40 mL) and refluxing time (24 h) were selected in the preparation of CDs.

TEM image of CDs indicated the good dispersion and nearly spherical shape with an average diameter at about 6.3 nm (Fig. S3A). XRD spectrum (Fig. S3B) displayed a broad peak at 2θ = 23.5°, corresponding to the (0 0 2) plane of graphite. In FT-IR (Fig. S3C), OH and NH stretching vibration (3309 cm−1), CH stretching vibrations (2924 and 2856 cm−1), CO, CC, and CN stretching vibrations (1690, 1624, 1582, 1488 and 1438 cm−1), CN and CO stretching vibrations (1364 and 1028 cm−1) were observed. Furthermore, XPS spectrum provided C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s typical peaks at 285, 400, and 531 eV with relative atomic percentages of C, N, and O at 80.9, 2.55, and 16.33% (Fig. S4A). The peaks at 284.7 (CC), 285.7 (CN/CC), 286.4 (CO), and 287.3 eV (CO/CN) in high-resolved C 1s spectrum (Fig. S4B), 531.6 (CO) and 533.0 eV (CO) in high-resolved O1s spectrum (Fig. S4C), 399.3 (graphitic-N/NH) and 400.1 eV (pyrrolic-N) in high-resolved N 1s spectrum (Fig. S4D) were observed. The results of XPS analysis were consistent well with those in FT-IR, which indicated that N was successfully doped in the framework of CDs, and the surface of CDs presented some functional groups such as –OH, –NH2, –COOH, and –pyrrole.

After 24 weeks of storage at 4 °C in PBS solution, fluorescence intensities of two emission peaks were seldom changed (Fig. S5A). Moreover, the influences of longtime UV light irradiation on the optical stability of CDs was investigated, and the results showed that the fluorescence intensity of CDs possessed excellent photostability under continuous irradiation at 365 nm for 90 min (Fig. S5B). In addition, fluorescence intensities of two emission peaks were nearly invariable at pH from 1.0 to 9.0 (Fig. S5C). Therefore, prepared CDs should be used as a good ratiometric sensor for sensing and bioimaging. In addition, quantum yield of CDs was calculated to be 10.4% using quinine sulfate as the reference (quantum yield 54% in 0.05 M H2SO4).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of off-on ratiometric CoA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: An “off-on” ratiometric fluorescence nanosensor was firstly established to sense CoA. At first, sixteen metal ions (40 μM) were separately added into CDs solution, and the original fluorescence intensity of CDs at 520 nm could be remarkably selectively quenched by Cu2+, while the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 650 nm seldom changed (Fig. S6A). Moreover, the response of CDs to Cu2+ is still very stable in the presence of other ions (Fig. S6B). These results indicated the selective response of CDs to Cu2+. The fluorescence intensities of CDs at 520 nm dropped gradually with increasing Cu2+ concentrations from 0 to 65 μM, and no obvious decrease happened when the concentrations of Cu2+ continuously increased from 65 to 80 μM; simultaneously, blue shifts of the maximum emission wavelength occurred (Fig. S7). After addition of Cu2+ (65 μM) into CDs solution (2.0 mg mL−1), the maximum UV absorption wavelengths (261, 310, and 343 nm) did not change, the absorption intensities decreased (Fig. S8A), and the fluorescence lifetime decreased from 4.31 to 4.19 ns (Fig. S8B). The results indicated that there existed affinity between surface groups of CDs (e.g. COOH, OH, NH2) and Cu2+, which induced electrostatic interaction, non-radiative electron transfer, and then led to the fluorescence quenching (Murugan, Prakash, Jayakumar, Sundaramurthy, & Sundramoorthy, 2019). CoA, containing thiol, phosphate and amino groups, could particularly conjugate with Cu2+ to form a more stable complex, and at this time, Cu2+ was decomposed from CDs, and the surface of CDs got free of Cu2+ (Fig. 1). Then, fluorescence of CDs was recovered. As expected, we observed a recovery of fluorescence intensity and maximum emission wavelength after adding CoA into CDs-Cu2+ solution (Fig. 3 A). Upon addition of CoA (150 μM), UV absorption intensity and fluorescence lifetime of the mixtures were almost the same with those of CDs (Fig. S9). Therefore, it could be concluded that CoA did not complex with CDs but Cu2+. Biothiols (e.g. Hcy, GSH, Cys) also had thiol group, and previous reports declared that biothiols had strong binding interactions with metal ions (e.g. Hg2+, Cu2+) to form stable S-Hg2+/S-Cu2+ complex (Liao, Li, Yang, & Chen, 2019). The thiol-responsive fluorescence sensors were less specific. It was noted that our prepared CDs-Cu2+ seldom responded to biothiols, not to say other amino acids without thiol group (Fig. S9). It could be speculated that CDs fabricated by white pepper complexed with Cu2+ stronger than biothiols, then biothiols could not restore the quenched fluorescence. So, white pepper-derived CDs could act as “off-on” ratiometric fluorescence sensors for the selective determination of CoA without any interference.

To achieve a highly sensitive detection of CoA, various conditions affecting the formation of CDs-Cu2+ complex (e.g. concentration of CDs and Cu2+, incubation pH and time), were investigated and optimized by according the F520/F668 values. As shown in Figs. S7 and S10, when the concentration of CDs, Cu2+, incubation pH and time were selected as 2 mg mL−1, 65 μM, 7.4 and 15 min, CDs showed higher fluorescence response and stability, and CDs-Cu2+ complex presented the lower fluorescence intensity. Finally, the quenched fluorescence in CDs-Cu2+ system could be recovered by addition of CoA within 5 min (Fig. S11). Under the optimized conditions, F520/F668 (y) showed a good linearity with the concentration of CoA (x) in the range of 0–150.0 μM (y = 0.018x + 1.96, R2 = 0.995) (Fig. 3B). The limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of CDs to CoA were 8.75 nM (3σ/k) and 28.89 nM (10σ/k), respectively. Compared with other analytical methods for detection of CoA, white pepper-derived CDs had an outstanding performance, such as simple and easy operation, high detection sensitivity, high selectivity, wide response range, and ratiometric detection (Table S1).

In order to explore the practicability of the white pepper-derived CDs, two pig liver samples were performed, and Fig. S12 showed the fluorescence spectra of them. With the calibration curve, concentrations of CoA in pig liver samples were found to be 0.12 ± 0.01 and 0.13 ± 0.01 µM (Table S2), which was coincided with previous report (Hu et al., 2017). Satisfactory recoveries (93.3–108.0%) of CoA in pig liver samples using the white pepper-derived CDs were achieved with the relative standard deviation (RSD) from 2.3 to 4.5% (Table S2). The results indicated white pepper-derived CDs exhibited a promising application in the detection of CoA in complex biological samples with high accuracy, high precision, and high selectivity.

Bio-source fluorescence sensors always showed low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility. MTT assay was employed to verify the high biocompatibility of white pepper-derived CDs. A serial of concentrations of CDs (0–1.0 mg mL−1) were incubated with HeLa cells for 24 h. Cell viability of Hela cells only declined about 5% at the high concentration of CDs (1.0 mg/mL) (Fig. S13), which indicated the low cytotoxicity and superior biocompatibility of white pepper-derived CDs to Hela cells. In vitro cellular uptake experiments in HeLa cells were then performed to detect CoA (Fig. 4 ). After incubation with CDs (1.0 mg/mL), HeLa cells exhibited green and red fluorescence under the 420 nm excitation wavelength, which indicated the white pepper-derived CDs has excellent compatibility with cell membrane (Fig. 4A). After the addition of Cu2+, green channel was obviously quenched while the red channel almost unchanged (Fig. 4B). For the detection of CoA, HeLa cells were firstly incubated with CoA and then CD-Cu2+, fluorescence for green channel was recovered, and red channel with no change was used a reference to image intracellular CoA (Fig. 4C). Cell imaging results agreed well with the fluorescence spectra. Therefore, the proposed white pepper-derived CDs have been shown to be excellent optical cell-imaging probes.

Imaging and determination of CoA in living cells and biological samples are of great importance for biomedical research and knowledge of the biofunctions of CoA. By comparison with single-emission CDs, ratiometric CDs offers more accuracy for bioimaging and bioanalysis by elimination of the effects of non-target factors. Results indicated that natural biomass was the ideal raw materials for facile fabrication of ratiometric CDs with novel functions. Moreover, the development of white pepper-derived ratiometric CDs offered a new platform for investigation of CoA.

A simple, green and low-cost synthesis protocol, using white pepper as raw material through refluxing in ethanol for 24 h, was developed to synthesize dual-emissive CDs for rapid, sensitive, selective ratiometric fluorescence detection of CoA in vitro and in vivo using “off-on” mode. The white pepper-derived CDs showed strong and stable photoluminescence, low cytotoxicity, superior biocompatibility, excellent performance to CoA, demonstrating their potential towards diverse applications.

Ruiqing Long: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Writing - original draft. Ying Guo: Project administration, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Lianwu Xie: Data curation, Formal analysis, Methodology. Shuyun Shi: Funding acquisition, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Jinju Xu: Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Chaoying Tong: Data curation, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Qinlu Lin: Investigation, Methodology. Te Li: Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660181), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2018JJ1043), and Key Laboratory of Hunan Province for Water Environment and Agriculture Product Safety (2018TP1003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2020.126171.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Alam et al., 2015

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: Excellent imaging agent for biomedical applications
Green Chemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Alam, A., Park, B., Ghouri, Z. K., Park, M., & Kim, H. (2015). Synthesis of carbon quantum dots from cabbage with down- and up-conversion photoluminescence properties: excellent imaging agent for biomedical applications. Green Chemistry, 17, 3791−3797.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging
Analytical Chimica Acta

Green and cost-effective synthesis of carbon dots from date kernel and their application as a novel switchable fluorescence probe for sensitive assay of Zoledronic acid drug in human serum and cellular imaging. Analytical Chimica Acta, 1030, 183−193.
